title: International tourism, number of arrivals
definition: International inbound tourists (overnight visitors) are the number of tourists who travel to a country other than that in which they have their usual residence, but outside their usual environment, for a period not exceeding 12 months and whose main purpose in visiting is other than an activity remunerated from within the country visited. When data on number of tourists are not available, the number of visitors, which includes tourists, same-day visitors, cruise passengers, and crew members, is shown instead. Sources and collection methods for arrivals differ across countries. In some cases data are from border statistics (police, immigration, and the like) and supplemented by border surveys. In other cases data are from tourism accommodation establishments. For some countries number of arrivals is limited to arrivals by air and for others to arrivals staying in hotels. Some countries include arrivals of nationals residing abroad while others do not. Caution should thus be used in comparing arrivals across countries. The data on inbound tourists refer to the number of arrivals, not to the number of people traveling. Thus a person who makes several trips to a country during a given period is counted each time as a new arrival.

source.sourceOrganization: Yearbook of Tourism Statistics, Compendium of Tourism Statistics and data files, UN Tourism

editorial.shortHook: A travel-flow map where small and famous destinations stand out.
editorial.patternNote: The darkest countries include France, United States, China, while the lightest include Solomon Islands, Guinea-Bissau, Sierra Leone; that contrast is the fastest way to read the tourism arrivals pattern.
editorial.whyItMatters: Tourism arrivals show global travel geography, transport access, and destination pull.

bestProbeCountries.reason: High anchor: this country sits near the darkest end of the map.
Low anchor: this country clarifies the lightest end of the scale.
Middle check: this country helps separate a true pattern from one-off outliers.

commonConfusions.reason: Air passengers carried can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
UHC service coverage can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
Container port traffic can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.

editorial.difficultyReason: Standard difficulty: the map has readable regional structure, but the best answer still depends on checking the unit and country anchors.
editorial.dataCaveat: Raw arrivals favor large or famous destinations and do not adjust for population.
regionalSignals: Economic-share maps are not the same as raw totals.
Small open economies can dominate percentage maps.

reviewNotes: Accessible and fun map with strong destination and transport clues.
Daily-worthy as long as reveal explains raw arrivals rather than per-capita tourism.
